coredata.dc:title: A facile synthesis of graphene nanoribbon-quantum dot hybrids and their application for composite electrolyte membrane in direct methanol fuel cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shukla, Avanish
Dhanasekaran, P.
Nagaraju, N.
Bhat, Santoshkumar D.
Pillai, Vijayamohanan K.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report the concept of synthesizing graphene nanoribbon-graphene quantum dot (GNR-GQD) hybrids with even grafting of GQD on GNR sheet in a facile single step process under ultrasonication in chlorosulfonic acid. Further, diazotization route is followed for the preparation of 4-benzenediazonium sulfonate precursor to sulfonate both GNR and GQD to form sulfonated graphene nanoribbons-sulfonated graphene quantum dots (sGNR-sGQD) nanohybrids. Synergistic and structure dependent effect of nanohybrids is seen via its dispersion in sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (sPEEK) to form nanocomposite membrane. sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane shows remarkable direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) performance compared to pristine sPEEK and Nafion 117 with 40% increment in peak power density along with higher durability up to 100 h due to better physicochemical properties like water uptake, ion exchange capacity, proton conductivity, and reduced methanol crossover to suggest its potential which includes diverse membrane applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanoribbon-graphene quantum dot hybrids
Nanohybrids
Sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone)
Nanocomposite membrane
Direct methanol fuel cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of membrane electrode assembly (MEA) and DMFC performance evaluation
Structural characterization of GNR, GNR-GQD and sGNR-sGQD
Characterization of pristine sPEEK and its nanocomposite PEM
DMFC performance evaluation and its durability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Bangladesh Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Mr. Avanish Shukla thank University Grant Commission ( UGC ), New Delhi, India and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research ( CSIR ), India for the CSIR-UGC research fellowship (under UGC fellowship scheme). Dr. Santoshkumar D. Bhat thank the project under CSIR -Young Scientist Award Research Grant ( DU-MLP-0090 ) for the funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images at different magnification of pristine GNR (a, b, c and d), GNR-GQD (e, f, g and h) and sGNR-sGQD (i, j, k and l). GNR-GQD images show the GQD (2–5 nm) embedded on GNR. The sGNR-sGQD image is also similar to GNR-GQD without any structural damage compared to pristine GNR.
Laser-Raman spectra of GNR, GNR-GQD and sGNR-sGQD.
FT-IR spectra for pristine GNR and sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid.
FE-SEM cross-sectional images of pristine sPEEK (a) and sPEEK/sGNR-GQD(1.5 wt %) nanocomposite PEM (b).
AFM image for pristine sPEEK (a and b) and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite PEM (c and d).
TGA of pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane.
The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) based MEA at 0.3 V at 80 °C in fuel cell mode.
DMFC performance of pristine sPEEK and composite membrane electrolytes along with Nafion 117 at 80 °C.
Durability of pristine sPEEK, sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite PEM and Nafion 117.
HR-TEM images for (a and b) pristine sPEEK and (c and d) sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) membrane electrolytes after 100 h durability test.
FT-IR spectra of the pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) membrane electrolytes after 100 h durability test.

Schematic illustration for the formation of GNR-GQD nanohybrid under ultrasonication using graphene nanoribbons (GNR) with chlorosulfonic acid via an epoxy intermediate which leads to the breakage of C-C bonds to form carboxylic acid and hydroxyl group. Further, diazotization route using 4-benzenediazonium sulfonate precursor for sulfonation of nanohybrid to form sGNR-sGQD.

Schematic representation of pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD nanocomposite membrane illustrating the generation of more ionic clusters/nanochannels (highly precise in nature) and blocking of pores through the incorporation of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid into the sPEEK matrix which enhances proton conduction (by acidic groups) and restricts the methanol permeation (by graphene backbone) in DMFC.
The methanol uptake and electrical conductivity of membrane samples.
Comparison of literature reports of carbon nanostructure-sPEEK composite membranes with the present study.

head.ce:title: A facile synthesis of graphene nanoribbon-quantum dot hybrids and their application for composite electrolyte membrane in direct methanol fuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute-MadrasUnit, CSIR Madras Complex, Chennai, 600113, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad, 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute-Madras Unit, CSIR Madras Complex, Chennai, 600113, India.
Corresponding author. CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi, 630003, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report the concept of synthesizing graphene nanoribbon-graphene quantum dot (GNR-GQD) hybrids with even grafting of GQD on GNR sheet in a facile single step process under ultrasonication in chlorosulfonic acid. Further, diazotization route is followed for the preparation of 4-benzenediazonium sulfonate precursor to sulfonate both GNR and GQD to form sulfonated graphene nanoribbons-sulfonated graphene quantum dots (sGNR-sGQD) nanohybrids. Synergistic and structure dependent effect of nanohybrids is seen via its dispersion in sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (sPEEK) to form nanocomposite membrane. sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane shows remarkable direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) performance compared to pristine sPEEK and Nafion 117 with 40% increment in peak power density along with higher durability up to 100 h due to better physicochemical properties like water uptake, ion exchange capacity, proton conductivity, and reduced methanol crossover to suggest its potential which includes diverse membrane applications.

Formation of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD nanocomposite membrane with high electrochemical selectivity through incorporation of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid into sPEEK matrix for DMFC application.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cell technology with its better energy conversion efficiency has attracted many researchers due to the availability of new materials facilitating its wider deployment over the last two decades being environmentally benign for sustainability [1]. Among all, the proton exchange membrane fuel cells is one of the most promising and efficient power generation systems [2,3]. Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFC) is considered to be one of the important fuel cells in the low temperature category particularly suitable for the portable applications due to the use of liquid methanol of high energy density, easy handling and storage capability with no requirement of external humidification unlike that of hydrogen based fuel cell systems [4–7]. The fabrication of integrated membrane electrode assembly (MEA) is important wherein polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) is a key component which determines properties like proton conductivity, mechanical strength and restricted fuel crossover [8–10]. Nafion®(a perfluorosulfonic acid ionomer) is commercially used as electrolyte in DMFC due to its high thermal, chemical and mechanical stability along with excellent proton conductivity [11–13]. However, it is not economically viable in DMFC applications due to its complicated manufacturing process along with significant amount of methanol crossover from anode to cathode creating the mixed potential [14–16]. Thus it is essential to use alternative PEMs for DMFC which addresses the above challenges associated with Nafion. Sulfonated aromatic polymers like sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (sPEEK) have emerged as one of the most suitable and potential replacements for Nafion being less costly with relatively low methanol crossover [17–22].

Poly(ether ether ketone) (PEEK) is semi-crystalline in nature which belongs to the poly(aryl ether ketone)s group. Chemically, PEEK has a repeating unit of oxy-1,4-phenylene-oxy-1,4-phenylenecarbonyl-1,4-phenylene [23]. PEEK can be easily sulfonated using sulfuric acid, sulfonic acid group is attached on the phenyl ring between two ether groups as the phenyl rings attached to carbonyl groups are deactivated by electronegative effect [14]. In terms of micro-structures and nano-channels for sPEEK, the hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions are less separated because of its rigid backbone [24]. The nano-channels are narrow, highly-branched having dead end channels in turn restricting the methanol crossover [24]. Further, sPEEK is a promising PEM for DMFC applications due to its excellent chemical, thermal and mechanical stability [2,25,26]. However, due to its higher degree of sulfonation in sPEEK, its mechanical property gets adversely affected causing a higher swelling and due to the presence of more dead end channels, the proton conductivity is also restricted [6,24,26]. Hence composite membranes of sPEEK are prepared by incorporating suitable additives like clays, zeolites, mesoporous molecular sieves, heteropolyacids, and other inorganic functionalized materials in order to overcome the above limitations [27–31]. These composites have better mechanical, thermal and chemical stability which could restrict the methanol permeability up to some extent, concomitantly enhancing the proton conducting networks in the composite electrolyte [14]. However efficient reduction in methanol permeability and durability of these composites at longer repetitive cycles in operation is always a problem leading to eventual degradation of performance.

In recent years, carbon nanostructures have been used to form many solid acid nanocomposites specifically for multifarious applications [32–34]. Among them, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), graphene and graphene oxide have been subjected as additives in Nafion and sPEEK for fuel cell applications because of their excellent mechanical, thermal and chemical stability which adds to the durability for the composite membranes in DMFC with restricted methanol permeability [35–39]. For instance, Nafion-sulfonated graphene oxide (s-GO), Nafion-sulfonated-graphene, Nafion-graphene oxide (GO), sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate-GO/sPEEK and s-GO/sPEEK are few important composite membranes for enhanced DMFC performance suggesting the utility of graphene as an additive [36,40–43]. Our earlier report on unzipped MWCNT to form graphene nanoribbons (GNR) and its interaction with sPEEK has suggested several desirable characteristics as electrolyte in polymer electrolyte fuel cells [44]. In a similar manner, hybrid electrolytes can also be designed for DMFC using selective modification as an essential prerequisite. This provides electrical conductivity control, better dispersion along with the enhanced interfacial interaction with sPEEK to form electrolyte in DMFC [45,46].

However, the design and utilization of structurally suitable and functionally compatible nanostructures are critical for these additives for performance enhancement along with the base polymer and the effective reduction in methanol crossover is possible only after specific modification of the ionic channels (generated after hydrophilic and hydrophobic interactions) without affecting its proton conductivity by incorporation of particulate nanomaterials with required size and shape. Although graphene has extraordinary mechanical and thermal properties [47–49], poor dispersion in different solvents along with aggregation cripples its benefit for further processing [50]. Consequently, chemical functionalization and processing of graphene in the form of nanohybrid is essential to make it compatible in solvent and also to form nanocomposite membranes. Thus the idea of synthesis of graphene nanoribbon-graphene quantum dot (GNR-GQD) hybrid may exhibit unique properties arising from the charge transfer and interactions (π-π and van der Walls) between them. GQD show optical and electrical properties, chemical inertness and low toxicity [51,52]. GQD have been synthesized using different top down approach viz. hydrothermal cutting, solvothermal cutting, electrochemical cutting, nanolithography, microwave-assisted cutting, and ultrasonic shearing [53–59]. Possible functionalization at their edges with its high solubility in different solvents facilitates the fabrication process for various applications [50,60].

In the present study, we subject GNR with less defect density by potassium vapor intercalation for synthesis of GNR-GQD nanohybrid and its modification to sulfonated graphene nanoribbons-sulfonated graphene quantum dots (sGNR-sGQD) as an additive in polymer electrolyte for direct methanol fuel cells. Further as sGNR-sGQD additives are sulfonated, there is a hydrogen bonding interaction between the sulfonic acid group of GNR and GQD. Effective size and shape of two nanomaterials sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid block the required amount of ionic channels without affecting its proton conducting paths in the sPEEK membrane. The introduction of such structurally dependent hybrid might be useful as an additive to synthesize the nanocomposite membrane of sPEEK specially for restricting methanol crossover in DMFC.

Graphene nanoribbons (≥90.0% carbon basis, L × W: 2–15 μm × 40–250 nm) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Chlorosulfonic acid was purchased from Merck, Germany. Sulfanilic acid, ACS reagent was purchased from Acros organics, USA (imported by Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Ltd.). Hypophosphorous acid (50% AR) was purchased from LobaChemie Pvt. Ltd. India. Ethyl alcohol was purchased from Hayman Group Ltd. UK. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sulfuric acid was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. India. sPEEK polymer fibers (fumion®E-600, IEC ∼ 1.65 meqg−1, Mw = 50,000 g mol−1, Mn = 14,000) was purchased from Fumatech BWT GmbH, Germany. DMFC Anode and Cathode was purchased from Alfa Aesar, Germany (imported by Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. India). N, N-Dimethyl Acetamide was purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. India. Methanol (Rankem) was purchased from Avantor performance Materials India limited, India. Deionized water (DI, MQ Res: 18.2 MΩcm) from Milli-Q was used for all the experiments.

Pristine GNR (200 mg) was mixed with 300 ml of chlorosulfonic acid and the mixture was ultra-sonicated (100 W) for 8 h. Then the mixture was slowly poured drop by drop in ice (1000 ml deionized (DI) water) (Note-chlorosulfonic acid is a super acid and the reaction with ice/water is extremely violent, so proper and intense care should be taken while performing these experiments). The mixture was repeatedly washed with DI water to remove residual acid and finally it was filtered and dried at 70 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The product GNR-GQD hybrid was then sulfonated using diazotization route. The 4-benzenediazoniumsulfonate precursor was synthesized by using sulfanilic acid via diazotization route as reported earlier [61]. Briefly, sulfanilic acid (5.2 g of 0.03 M) was dispersed in 300 ml of 1M HCl solution in a three neck round bottom flask. Further the flask was moved to ice bath to maintain the temperature in the range of 3–5 °C with continuous stirring and then 33 ml of 1 M NaNO2 aqueous solution was added dropwise to get a clear solution. The mixture was allowed to be stirred under same condition for 1 h to get a white precipitate. The white precipitate was filtered and washed with a copious amount of cold DI water (Note- 4-Benzenediazoniumsulfonate is volatile in nature at room temperature so care should be taken to maintain the low temperature surroundings, required while filtration and transfer to the solution of second step to avoid any loss). In the second step, 180 mg of GNR-GQD was mixed with 60 ml water and 60 ml ethanol and was sonicated for 1 h to get a dispersed solution. Then the solution was kept in an ice bath for 15 min to obtain 3–5 °C under stirring. In the next step, the white precipitate of 4-benzenediazoniumsulfonate from above step was mixed to this solution and was allowed to be stirred for next 10 min. 60 ml of hypophosphorous acid was added dropwise to the solution under continuous stirring. After 30 min, again 60 ml of hypophosphorous acid was added dropwise and the solution was allowed to be stirred maintaining the same condition of 3–5 °C for another 3 h. Finally the solution was filtered several times with DI water and then the final filtered product namely sGNR-sGQD was dried at 70 °C in vacuum oven for 12 h.

Solution casting technique was used to prepare nanocomposite PEM of sPEEK and sGNR-sGQD (i.e. sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD) in different wt. % ratio (viz., 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0) of additive in relation to polymer. For comparison, sulfonated graphene nanoribbon (sGNR) (1.5 wt %) is also incorporated in pristine sPEEK to form composite membrane of sPEEK/sGNR. In brief, the required amount of additive was sonicated in dimethyl acetamide (DMAc) for 2 h and 5 wt % sPEEK solution was prepared by dissolving 1 g of sPEEK in 20 ml of DMAc, finally both the solutions were mixed and was stirred and sonicated continuously for 6 h. The solution was poured on a flat glass Petri dish of diameter 10 cm and was dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven for 18 h. The pristine sPEEK was also prepared similarly without the additive. The membranes were treated with 0.5 M sulfuric acid for 4 h to activate the proton conducting ionic channels and then it was washed thoroughly with DI water to remove surface acidity before its further application. The membrane thickness was in the range of 160–180 μm similar to Nafion 117 for comparison.

Different characterization techniques were used to confirm the formation of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid in conjunction with composite membrane characterization. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed to study the size and shape along with elemental mapping for distribution of carbon, oxygen and sulphur of pristine GNR, GNR-GQD and sGNR-sGQD after drop cast of a solution made up of ethanol-water mixture, and the membrane samples after the drop cast of a solution by dissolving the membranes in dimethylformamide on a TEM grid (TED Pella, Inc.,USA) on a Tecnai G2 200 kV FEG TMP, FEI. Laser-Raman was performed to study the quality of nanohybrid on Renishaw Invia Raman microscope (Renishaw, U.K.) using He-Ne Laser 633 nm, 18 mW. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) of GNR and sGNR-sGQD was performed after drop casting of a solution in ethanol-water mixture on a silicon wafer substrate (Sigma-Aldrich) on AFM (Bruker, MultiMode8) and also the AFM was performed for pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane to study the surface roughness profile in tapping mode atomic force microscopy (AFM, Pico SPM-Picoscan2100, Molecular Imaging, USA). The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy for pristine GNR and sGNR-sGQD; pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) membranes after 100 h durability test was carried out on a TENSOR 27 (Bruker optik GmbH, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out for GNR and sGNR-sGQD nanocomposite on Thermo Scientific MULTILAB 2000 Base system with X-Ray, Auger and ISS attachments. The thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) of GNR and sGNR-sGQD along with pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) PEM was performed in the nitrogen environment (flow: 60 ml min−1) with heating rate of 5 °C min−1 within temperature range of 30 °C and 900 °C on a NETZSCH STA 449F3 TGA-DSC instrument.

The surface morphology of pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membranes; membrane samples i.e. sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (2.0 wt %) after drop cast of a solution by dissolving the membranes in dimethylformamide on copper stub was studied on field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) instrument (TESCAN MIRA3 LMU), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis for GNR and sGNR-sGQD was performed on Oxford instruments (X-act, PentaFET precision) connected to above mentioned FE-SEM instrument. The elemental analysis (CHNS) for the GNR and sGNR-sGQD was performed in Elementarvario EL 111- Germany. Proton conductivity measurements were performed in a Bekktech membrane conductivity cell setup kept inside a bench-top type temperature & humidity chamber (ESPEC, SH-242) connected with Biologic science instruments (model: SP-150). The wettability study (water contact angle measurement) of the membranes by the 8 μl water droplet was measured by optical contact angle meter (OCA 35, dataphysics). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at 0.3 V at 80 °C in fuel cell mode is performed on electrochemical work station (Biologic VSP, France) attached with 20A booster (Biologic, VMP3B-20). Tensile strength and % elongation at break for pristine sPEEK and its nanocomposite membrane samples after dipping in water for 24 h was determined by universal testing machine (UTM) (model: ZWICK/Roell, 146500) using ASTMD882 with 10 mm width and 25 mm grip to grip separation of membrane samples. The testing was performed on a load cell of 1 kN with the test speed of 5 mm min−1.

Ion exchange capacity (IEC) measurement for membrane samples was carried out using acid-base titration method as reported earlier [44]. A membrane sample of 2 cm2 was immersed for 48 h in NaCl solution (saturated), and membrane sample was then taken out and the remaining solution was titrated with NaOH solution. IEC values was calculated by equation (1)
(1)
IEC =
Volum
e
NaOH
×
Normalit
y
NaOH
Dry
weight
of
sample
meq .
g
− 1
Water uptake measurement was carried out by saturating the dried membrane sample of 2 cm2 area in DI water for 24 h [44]. The weight of dried membrane and wet membrane samples were noted down after removal of surface water gently and water uptake values were calculated by equation (2). The methanol uptake measurement is carried out similarly using 2 M methanol solution as a substitute to water.
(2)
Water
and
methanol
uptake
(
%
)
=
W
sat .
−
W
dry
W
dry
× 100
Where W
sat. and W
dry represents the saturated weight after 24 h in water and dry weight of the membrane samples respectively.

Proton conductivity measurements for the membrane samples of size of length 2 cm and width of 0.5 cm were carried out at different temperatures under fully humidified conditions and impedance was measured for all samples after equilibration for 1 h at each temperature. The Ohmic resistance of the membrane sample was estimated from the Nyquist plot by the high frequency intercept and finally proton conductivity was calculated by equation (3).
(3)
Membrane
conductivity =
L
R . A
Sc
m
− 1
Where L is the distance between two platinum electrodes in the Bekktech membrane conductivity cell set up which is 0.5 cm, R is the resistance obtained from Nyquist impedance and A is the cross-sectional area of membrane sample.

The Activation energy (Ea) required for the proton transport for the membranes is calculated by Arrhenius equation in equation (4)[44].
(4)
ln
σ = ln
σ
0
−
E
a
RT
Where σ is the proton conductivity (mScm−1), σ0 is pre-exponential factor (mScm−1), R is gas constant, T is the temperature (K).

Electrical conductivity of the completely dried membranes is also measured similarly as above without subjecting it to any humidification at 80 °C to find the electrical conductivity induced by the nanohybrids additive in the composite membrane.

Methanol permeability of membrane samples was calculated using two compartment diffusion cell setup with continuous stirring on both sides at 30 °C. In diffusion cell setup, one side is filled with 2 M methanol solution designated as compartment A and other side is filled with DI water and the membrane sample was clamped in middle separating the two compartments for methanol diffusion due to concentration gradient from compartment A to compartment B. The methanol concentration in compartment B was referred as CB and it was measured with respect to time for 8 h using gas chromatography (Thermo scientific Trace GC-700) equipped with capillary column and flame ionization detector (FID). The methanol permeability values were calculated by equation (5)
(5)
P =
k
B
×
V
B
×
L
(
C
A
−
C
B
)
×
A
c
m
2
s
− 1
Where P is the methanol permeability of the membrane, kB is the slope of the concentration profile with respect to time of compartment B, VB is the volume in compartment B and CA & CB is the concentration of compartment A and B respectively after 8 h. L is the thickness of membrane and A is the actual area of the membrane sample exposed for methanol crossover.

Electrochemical selectivity (β) of membrane was determined using ratio of proton conductivity and methanol permeability as shown in equation (6).
(6)
β
=
Proton
conductivity
Methanol
permeability
Sc
m
− 3
s

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of membrane electrode assembly (MEA) and DMFC performance evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: MEAs were prepared by uni-axially sandwiching the membrane in between the electrodes and then compacting under a load of 15 kg cm−2 pressure at 80 °C for 2 min on a hydraulic press (Flow Mech). The anode comprising Pt-Ru/C and cathode comprising Pt/C with a loading of 2 mg cm−2 on each side was used as electrodes. DMFC performance evaluation was tested using the prepared MEAs in a fuel cell setup having serpentine flow field on the graphite plates (Fuel Cell Technology, USA) with an active area of 5 cm2. The MEAs were carefully placed in between the two bipolar plates using a gasket of proper thickness. Further, the cell was activated for first day at Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) for 30 min using 2 ml min−1 flow of methanol (2M) on anode side as fuel and 250 ml min−1 flow rate of oxygen at ambient pressure on cathode side as oxidant using electronic load model-LCN4-25-24/LCN 50-24 from Bitrode Instruments (USA). The test was continued for the very next day using the same flow rate of fuel for polarization at 80 °C after activating the cell in galvanostatic mode by dragging a constant current from the cell to observe the corresponding value of voltage.

Durability of the MEAs comprising of pristine sPEEK, sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) and Nafion 117 was evaluated intermittently for 100 h at 80 °C. Durability was performed at OCV condition in relation with time at the same flow rate of fuel as mentioned above for the polarization. The durability test was performed at start-stop cycle for 10 h every day up to 100 h. HR-TEM and FT-IR characterization of membranes after 100 h durability test was carried to understand structural and morphology changes of polymer and nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GNR, GNR-GQD and sGNR-sGQD
ce:section.ce:para: The structural and edge effect properties of GNR are unique although the exact details depend on its preparation via different routes using exfoliation of MWCNT and graphite [62]. It is important to note that harsh oxidative unzipping of MWCNT using strong acid and oxidizing agent may result in damage of surface structure and edge structure in GNR and hence the quality of GNR will be adversely affected [46,62,63]. However, GNR synthesized by potassium vapor intercalation in MWCNT results in better quality without any structural damage [64]. GNR with long ribbon structure have the advantage of high aspect ratio, whereas the GQD have unique properties due to its quantum confinement (in the range of diameter of few nanometres). Hence it would be naturally interesting to investigate a combination of these two materials for any synergistic effects in ionic transport.

Synthesis of GNR-GQD nanohybrid from GNR is carried out by efficient and facile synthesis using concentrated chlorosulfonic acid. The schematic of step-wise formation of sGNR-sGQD is represented in Scheme 1 . Chlorosulfonic acid being a super acid, can be used for exfoliation of graphite to few layer graphene [65]. It strongly protonates the edges of pristine GNR and induces the electrostatic repulsive forces and also being strong oxidizing agent it oxidizes the GNR double bond near edges and basal planes. The process of protonation and functionalization on the edges and on the basal planes might result in generation of unpaired electrons adjacent to the reactive point leading to a chain reaction to propagate and form more stable quantum confined structure of quantum dots, which are more prone on the reactive edges compared to the basal plane surface area. Further, the defects are considered as the additional reactive sites for the functionalization and hence the formation of GQD [46,63,66]. The ultrasonication enhances this process due to mechanical strain which facilitates the exfoliation and chain propagation. Hence the extent of synthesis of GQD in the GNR-GQD nanohybrid depends on the time for ultrasonication. The formation of GQD is also expected to be enhanced through the oxidizing radicals such as peroxyl radicals due to ultrasonication in presence of air [46,67] suggesting a long period, thus reducing the size of graphene layers.

A comparison of the HR-TEM images in Fig. 1 e, f, g and h, shows circular dot like structures which indicates the formation of GQD (2–5 nm) embedded on GNR designated as GNR-GQD nanohybrid in comparison to Fig. 1a, b, c and d where pristine GNR are observed. It is also seen for GNR-GQD nanohybrid, that the structure and edges of GNR remain the same and is not disrupted. Further, sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid was synthesized after sulfonation of GNR-GQD seen in Fig. 1i, j, k, and l, indicating the straight edges of GNR in sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid.

Fig. 2 shows the Raman spectra of GNR, GNR-GQD and sGNR-sGQD. The ratio of intensity (I) of diamondoid (D) to graphitic (G) (ID/IG), where D band is due to the characteristic of sp3 carbon content and G band is related to vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms suggests the defect density, disorder, edge structure and smoothness [65,68–71]. The ID/IG ratio of 0.45 as seen in Fig. 2for pristine GNR indicate the low defect pristine GNR, whereas the value of 0.31 for GNR-GQD suggest that there is no defect introduced in the starting material as well as the formation of GNR-GQD nanohybrid. Further after sulfonation, the ID/IG ratio of sGNR-sGQD increased to 0.36 suggesting the functionalization, due to the increase in sp3 domains on the surface and the edges of GNR-GQD after sulfonation.

As shown in Fig. S1of the supporting information, the ribbon structures with smooth edges are evident from AFM image of pristine GNR (a and b), GNR-GQD (c and d) and sGNR-sGQD (e and f). The average maximum height of 3.17 nm for pristine GNR, 2.88 nm for GNR-GQD and 1.79 nm for sGNR-sGQD suggests the gradual decrement in the height profile which is evident for the decrement in the layer thickness for the nanohybrid along with the formation of GQD. The above point is also validated by the roughness value of 4.88 nm for pristine GNR, 4.57 nm for GNR-GQD and 2.70 nm for sGNR-sGQD.

FT-IR analysis is performed in order to confirm the functionalization of GNR-GQD nanohybrid. Accordingly, Fig. 3 represents a comparison of the FT-IR spectra for pristine GNR and sGNR-sGQD. The peak at 1639.4 cm−1 in GNR and 1595.0 cm−1 in sGNR-sGQD represents the graphitic nature due to CC stretching. The pronounced peak in the FT-IR spectra of sGNR-sGQD at 2339.5 cm−1 and 2410.9 cm−1 is assigned to O-H stretch peak of carboxylic acid group [72] which arises after the oxidative cleavage of GQD from GNR and further present on the edges of GQD. The less pronounced peak in pristine GNR at the same wavelength suggests the residual carboxylic acid groups. The peak at 2923.9 cm−1 in GNR and sGNR-sGQD represents the asymmetric stretch of graphitic C C-H [72]. The pronounced peaks at 1161.1 cm−1 and 1294.1 cm−1 represents the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of OSO for sGNR-sGQD respectively suggesting the sulfonation. The EDX analysis also confirms the sulfonation as seen in Fig. S2of the supporting information, sulphur and oxygen content of sGNR-sGQD increased to 0.50 at. % and 4.85 at. % compared to sulphur and oxygen content of 0 at. % and 2.23 at. % respectively for pristine GNR. Also, the elemental mapping from HR-TEM of sGNR-sGQD in Fig. S3hof the supporting information shows higher oxygen content compared to pristine GNR of Fig. S3dand further Fig. S3ishows sulfur content in sGNR-sGQD indicating sulfonation.

Fig. S4of the supporting information shows comparative XP survey and its high resolution spectra for GNR and sGNR-sGQD. As seen in survey spectra (Fig. S4a), the carbon and oxygen are present in C1s, O1s state of GNR and sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid and further sulphur is present in S2p state in sGNR-sGQD. The characteristic binding energy for GNR for different C1s state are C-C (284.6 eV); CC (285.7 eV); C-O (286.7 eV); CO (286.7 eV) [73–76] and O1s are CO (531.9 eV), C–O–C, C-O-H (533.9 eV), (530.0 eV) [77]. Whereas in sGNR-sGQD, the different states of C1s state are C-C (284.6 eV); CC (285.8 eV); C-O (287.1 eV); CO (287.1 eV) [73–75], O1s state are CO (532.0 eV), C–O–C, C-O-H (533.9 eV), (530.1 eV) [77]. In addition, different states of S2p are S2p3/2 (168.8 eV) and S2p1/2(170.0 eV) with a separation of 1.2 eV [44]. The functional groups are similar in both GNR and sGNR-sGQD for C1s and O1s state. Further the presence of S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 peak in sGNR-sGQD confirms the sulfonation [78].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pristine sPEEK and its nanocomposite PEM
ce:section.ce:para: The sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid as an additive is dispersed in pristine sPEEK to improve the physico-chemical properties. The sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid interaction with sPEEK polymer is through hydrogen bonding between the sulfonic acid groups of nanohybrids and sulfonated polymer. Also the hydrophilic and hydrophobic interaction between the nanohybrid and sPEEK polymer in composite membrane is apparent as the backbone of additive is hydrophobic which interacts with sulfonic acid group. The incorporation of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid in pristine sPEEK has the significant impact on the properties of the polymer as supported by results from different characterization techniques. For comparison under identical condition, sulfonated graphene nanoribbon (sGNR) (1.5 wt %) is also incorporated in pristine sPEEK to form sPEEK/sGNR nanocomposite membrane.

Tensile strength and elongation at break (%) are studied for the PEM to determine the mechanical stability. As represented in Table 1 , the tensile strength and elongation at break (%) for the nanocomposite membrane with the sGNR-sGQD content of 1.0 wt % increased marginally in comparison to that of pristine sPEEK due to the compatibility and reinforcement of the additive with pristine sPEEK base polymer. However, the maximum mechanical reinforcement attained by any additive in a base polymer is limited to a specific content/wt. % of the additive resulting in a nanocomposite membrane. So as the additive content increased to 1.5 wt %, the tensile strength and elongation at break (%) decreased marginally in comparison to 1.0 wt %, this is due to the threshold additive content limit of 1.0 wt % required for the mechanical reinforcement and the large-scale phase separation between the sPEEK and additive above 1.0 wt % [79]. On the other hand, the tensile strength and elongation at break of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) are also comparable to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) attributed to the similar content of graphene based additive. Further, with additive content of 2.0 wt %, both tensile strength and elongation at break (%) decreased due to the agglomeration which has its adverse effect on the mechanical reinforcement. It is to be noted that the amount of additive content is not inducing the rigidity to the nanocomposite membrane and the tensile strength is approximately similar to pristine sPEEK. It is important to note that Nafion 117 has lower tensile strength among all the other composite PEM mentioned above but has a higher value of elongation at break (%) attributed to the strong fluorinated backbone of Nafion.

Fig. 4 a shows comparative FE-SEM cross-sectional images of pristine sPEEK revealing plane rough surface whereas Fig. 4b for sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) confirms the formation of nanocomposite membrane with nanohybrids as an additive, i.e. sGNR-sGQD in a uniform network arrangement. As seen in Fig. S5a and bof supporting information, FE-SEM images also confirm the agglomeration of nanohybrids at 2.0 wt % additive content in the nanocomposite membrane, since sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (2.0 wt %) composite membrane has more dense ribbons compared to the sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) membrane. Typical HR-TEM image (Fig. S5cof supporting information) also confirms the same for sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (2.0 wt %) composite membrane.

The AFM was carried out for the PEM to determine the surface roughness along with distribution of additive in polymer. Fig. 5 a and b for pristine sPEEK at a scale of 5 μm shows the roughness profile of mean height of 6 nm, whereas the image of Fig. 5c and d for the nanocomposite membrane shows few ribbons on the surface with the mean height of 42 nm. These results of AFM suggest the incorporation of sGNR-sGQD as an additive in the pristine sPEEK. The presence of additive on the surface of the nanocomposite PEM is evident from the AFM image and is important for the restriction of methanol crossover as coverage of active membrane area by these nanoparticles will reduce the methanol crossover due to the inert and hydrophobic behaviour of the graphene backbone where methanol follow tortuous path in comparison to pristine membrane. Whereas due to the sulfonation of sGNR-sGQD, the proton conductivity is not hindered. Further as seen in AFM images in Fig. S6of supporting information, larger size ribbons are clearly revealed in sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (2.0 wt %) composite membrane, formed due to the agglomeration.

As evident from the TGA in Fig. 6 , that the sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane has higher thermal stability than pristine sPEEK which is more pronounced after 300 °C, since sGNR-sGQD hybrids are thermally stable, provides improved stability to composite membrane due to the hydrogen bonding interactions with sulfonic acid groups of sPEEK. Degradation profile pattern is almost similar in both the cases. The first step degradation loss is around 15% due to the release of water molecules in temperature range (0–120 °C) for sPEEK and its nanocomposites. The second step loss around 10% and 5% is due to the degradation of sulfonic acid group in sPEEK and its nanocomposites in the temperature range (300–350 °C). The third step loss around 480–800 °C is due to the decomposition of the polymer backbone. The third step degradation loss is lower in case of nanocomposite membrane due to the presence of thermally stable sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid in the matrix.

The ion exchange capacity is an important parameter for effective functioning of a polymer electrolyte membrane, since it suggests the capacity for proton exchange related to its acidic content. As seen in Table 1, IEC of the PEMs suggest that the nanocomposite membrane with 1.0 wt % sGNR-sGQD increased i.e. 1.10 meqgm−1 in comparison to 0.96 of pristine sPEEK. Further it is significantly increased to 1.30 meqgm−1 with 1.5 wt % sGNR-sGQD which is an optimized additive content, attributed to the addition of sulfonated hybrid, responsible for the increment in the acidic content. The IEC of nanocomposite membrane with 2.0 wt % additive content decreased in comparison to 1.5 wt % due to the agglomeration above the optimized weight % and so the exposed/effective sulfonated region of the additive is reduced and compatibility with the base polymer is decreased but still the IEC is higher compared to that of pristine sPEEK. It is presumed that above the optimized content of 1.5 wt %, the total hydrophobic region (graphene backbone) of the 2.0 wt % additive content is dominant over the hydrophilic region (sulfonic acid content) due to the agglomeration. The IEC of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) is 1.16 meqgm−1, which is lower in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) due to the more acidic (sulfonic and carboxylic) groups available in sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid, wherein the additional contribution is from the edges of sGQD. The IEC of the Nafion 117 was measured to the lowest value of 0.72 meqgm−1.

As shown in Table 1, the water uptake of nanocomposite membrane increased with 1.0 wt % and 1.5 wt % sGNR-sGQD compared to pristine sPEEK. This is attributed to the additional sulfonic acid groups attached to GNR-GQD due to which sorption of water molecules is higher [44]. Further at higher wt. % of 2.0, the decrement in water uptake is seen compared to 1.5 wt % due to the agglomeration of sGNR-sGQD in the nanocomposite membrane [44]. The water uptake of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) is lower in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane, due to lower acidic content associated with sGNR in comparison to sGNR-sGQD. In comparison, Nafion 117 has the lowest water uptake in comparison to all the other PEMs due to the less amount of sulfonic acid group present compared to sPEEK [24].

Methanol solution induced stress for the membranes is critical for considering its degradation during fuel cell operation. As represented in Table 2 , methanol uptake of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.0 wt %) and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) slightly increased compared to pristine sPEEK which is due to sulfonic acid group enriched GNR-GQD. It is to be noted that methanol uptake for these composite membranes are slightly higher than pristine sPEEK. However methanol cross-over for the nanocomposite membranes during the DMFC operation is lower since methanol holding capacity of the nanocomposite membranes is higher compared to pristine sPEEK due to the presence of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid. In contrast, methanol uptake for Nafion is lower due to lesser acidic content but the methanol cross-over is higher due to the larger ionic clusters. Pristine sPEEK has higher methanol uptake and lower methanol cross-over than Nafion due to the high acidic content and dead end ionic channels respectively [24]. Further the methanol uptake of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (2.0 wt %) is reduced due to agglomeration of additive and so free sulfonic acid group is not present to take part in methanol sorption. The swelling of the membranes due to methanol solution during operation of fuel cell causes stress which may lead to its degradation in longer run.

Similarly as shown in Table 1, the proton conductivity of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.0 wt %) nanocomposite membrane increases at 30 °C and 80 °C compared to pristine sPEEK. Further increment of ionic conductivity at 1.5 wt % sGNR-sGQD is attributed to the sulfonic acid group present which broadens the ionic cluster size along with the conversion of dead end channels of pristine sPEEK to form new proton conducting sites in the nanocomposite membrane [24] as represented in Scheme 2 . It is to be noted that the size of the ionic channels of sPEEK is estimated to be 4 nm [6] which is similar to the sGQD size (2–5 nm), so it is expected that the quantum dots (free) are effectively able to orient itself in the ionic nano-channels so as to fine tune the ionic conducting channels through hydrogen bonding and hydrophilic-hydrophobic interaction. The proton conducting mechanism in sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD nanocomposite PEM follows both GrÖtthuss and Vehicular type. The sGNR-sGQD nanoadditive introduces the new proton hopping positions resulting in GrÖtthuss type mechanism and more adsorbed water content in the membrane acts as protonic charge carrier resulting in vehicular type mechanism [80–82]. Further at a higher wt. % (2.0), the proton conductivity is less in comparison to the 1.5 wt % which is due to the aggregation of sGNR-sGQD nanoparticles in sPEEK [44]. The phenomenon of aggregation might happen when the additive is above its optimum content subsequent to which, the compatibility between the additive and the base polymer is lost. The proton conductivity of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) membrane is lower in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) which is attributed to the lower surface enriched sulfonic acid groups to GNR in comparison with GNR-GQD hybrid.

As seen in Fig. 7 for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Ohmic resistance is derived from the high frequency intercept on real axis (x-axis). The Ohmic resistance of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) is lower (shown in inset) in comparison to pristine sPEEK suggesting better ionic transport for the composite membranes. It is to be noted that the amount of additive dispersed in the nanocomposite membrane is within the electrical percolation threshold limit and also the electrical conductivity of materials decrease due to the bonding of free electrons during functionalization (oxidation and sulfonation). It is found that the electrical conductivity of all the composite membranes along with pristine sPEEK is in the same range and there is no increase in electrical conductivity as represented in Table 2.

As shown in Table 1, low activation energy is required for proton transport with the nanocomposite membrane comprising 1.0 wt % sGNR-sGQD in comparison with sPEEK and further for optimized additive content of 1.5 wt %, it significantly decreased facilitating improved proton conduction. Further at increased content of 2.0 wt %, activation energy increased due to the agglomeration of additive hindering the ionic transport. The activation energy of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) is more in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) due to the lower proton conductivity of the former. This is in complete agreement with the literature [44].

Hydrophilic or hydrophobic properties of the membranes are analysed through water contact angle measurement (wettability study) as shown in Fig. S7of supporting information. It is found that the composite with 1.0 wt % sGNR-sGQD has higher hydrophilicity compared to pristine sPEEK and further 1.5 wt % of sGNR-sGQD has shown higher hydrophilicity than 1.0 wt % which is attributed to the sulfonic acid groups present in sGNR-sGQD. The hydrophilicity of 2.0 wt % sGNR-sGQD decreased compared to 1.5 wt % & 1.0 wt % due to its agglomeration in the sPEEK matrix.

Restriction of methanol crossover in DMFC is essential for better performance and long term durability. As seen in Table 1, the methanol crossover for 1.0 wt % of sGNR-sGQD decreased significantly and further at 1.5 wt % it decreased to 50% of the value for pristine sPEEK which is attributed to the methanol restricting properties and proper dispersion of sGNR-sGQD in sPEEK. The methanol crossover is presumed mainly via pores of the membrane and the nanohybrid sGNR-sGQD block the pores. The reduction in methanol crossover in composite membrane is due to the graphene's chemical inertness and good barrier property [36]. Methanol traverses through a tortuous path of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid which holds the methanol in its framework to restrict the cross-over through the composite membrane. Since the sPEEK ionic channels are also narrow, methanol restricting capability is higher compared to that of Nafion [24] and further the network of nanohybrid restrict the unreacted methanol to pass through the channels as represented in Scheme 2. However at 2.0 wt % of the additive, the methanol crossover restriction efficiency is less in comparison to the optimized level of 1.5 wt %, due to the agglomeration of the additive in the polymer. It is presumed that due to the excess content of sGNR-sGQD, compatibility between sPEEK and nanohybrid decreased above the optimized level resulting in higher methanol permeability by creating separation between ionic phases wherein methanol cross-over may happen. The methanol crossover of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) is more in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) as the smaller pores of sPEEK are not effectively blocked in comparison to later. As seen in Scheme 2, the nanocomposite PEM shows the generation of more ionic clusters/nanochannels (highly precise in nature) through the incorporation of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid into the sPEEK matrix which enhances proton conduction and restricts the methanol permeation in DMFC. However for Nafion 117, electroosmotic drag of water along with methanol is higher due to its larger ionic clusters [24]. Electrochemical selectivity is a very important parameter validated by the ratio of proton conductivity and methanol crossover. As seen in Table 1, electrochemical selectivity at additive sGNR-sGQD content of 1.0 wt % increased compared to that of pristine sPEEK which is significant at 1.5 wt % i.e. 2.5 times higher than pristine sPEEK, indicating the electrochemical selectivity of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid in pristine sPEEK with better ionic conductivity. Further at higher additive wt. % of 2.0, the electrochemical selectivity decreases in comparison to 1.5 wt % but is higher than pristine sPEEK. The electrochemical selectivity of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) is significantly lesser than sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) which is due to lower proton conductivity and higher methanol crossover of sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %). Electrochemical selectivity of Nafion 117 is lower than that of the optimized 1.5 wt % of sGNR-sGQD due to the higher methanol permeability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DMFC performance evaluation and its durability
ce:section.ce:para: sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid-sPEEK matrix based MEAs show increased DMFC performance and long-term durability. As shown in Fig. 8 , the nanocomposite with sGNR-sGQD content of 1.5 wt % depicts the highest performance (peak power density) of 142 mW cm−2 at a current density of 420 mA cm−2 which is 40% higher compared to that of pristine sPEEK of 100 mW cm−2 at 320 mA cm−2. The performance of 1.5 wt % nanocomposite membrane is followed by the additive content of 1.0 wt % with 132 mW cm−2 and 2.0 wt % with 124 mW cm−2. The DMFC performance with the sGNR-sGQD content of 2.0 wt % is decreased compared to 1.5 wt % due to the additive agglomeration after exceeding the optimized content in the nanocomposite membrane. The DMFC peak power density is 118 mW cm−2 for sPEEK/sGNR (1.5 wt %) which is lower in comparison to sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %). The DMFC performance of all the nanocomposites is well justified with physico-chemical properties in Table 1. The bench mark membrane Nafion 117 shows the peak power density of 80 mW cm−2 at 300 mA cm−2 which is comparatively lower than all the PEMs due to the higher methanol crossover in Nafion 117.

The durability of MEA is an essential requirement of DMFC which is usually hampered due to the significant amount of methanol crossover from anode to cathode through the membrane which is significant at open circuit voltage (OCV) and hence the durability is carried out at OCV condition. In this sequence, the sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid has been used as the efficient additive which is able to restrict the methanol crossover significantly. As seen in Fig. 9 , the sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane has shown better durability up to 100 h over pristine sPEEK and Nafion 117. The nanocomposite membrane has OCV of 0.66 V after 100 h which is higher compared to that of pristine sPEEK (0.64 V) and Nafion 117 (0.63 V). The higher durability of sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) after 100 h test in comparison with that of pristine sPEEK and Nafion 117 is attributed to the restricted methanol crossover with better thermal stability due to the presence of sGNR-sGQD. Further, OCV recovered during every start (during start-stop cycle for 10 h every day till 100 h) which is attributed to the activation of triple phase boundary in the catalyst layer. Methanol crossover is the main reason for OCV drop and if the cell is kept for 12 h, the remaining methanol on cathode side gets oxidized. After the process, OCV again recovers during the start cycle. HR-TEM and FT-IR characterizations are carried out for the nanocomposite membrane and pristine sPEEK after 100 h durability test. Fig. 10 of HR-TEM images reveal that there is no defect in structure and morphology for the sGNR-sGQD hybrid which is attributed to the chemical inertness, thermal stability and mechanical strength of the nanohybrid.

FT-IR of pristine sPEEK and sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) after the 100 h durability test is represented in Fig. 11 . The pronounced peak at 1521.5 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the CC stretch of aromatic backbone of sPEEK as well as sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid [83]. The pronounced peak at 1307.4 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the C-O-C stretching of the epoxy group of sPEEK and CO bend of the carbonyl group of sPEEK as well as the functional groups attached to the sGNR-sGQD [84]. The peaks at 1677.7 cm−1 and 1745.2 cm−1represents CO stretching of carbonyl group of sPEEK and the functional groups of sGNR-sGQD in composite membrane [83]. The peak at 2317.9 cm−1 and 2406.6 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the O-H stretch peak of carboxylic acid group of sGNR-sGQD additive [72]. The pronounced peak at 941.1 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the SO stretching of sulfonic acid groups of sPEEK and sGNR-sGQD [84]. The pronounced peak at 1180.2 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the OSO asymmetric stretching of the sulfonic acid groups of sPEEK and sGNR-sGQD. The peak at 2994.8 cm−1 represents the CC-H asymmetric stretch of hydrogen attached to aromatic ring in sPEEK as well as the sGNR-sGQD [72]. The peak at 3606.1 cm−1 represents the O-H stretching of sulfonic acid group of both the sPEEK and sGNR-sGQD [85]. The peak at 3835.6 cm−1 in composite membrane represents the O-H stretch of adsorbed water. It is to be noted that prominent peaks are retained for sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD (1.5 wt %) nanocomposite membrane in comparison with pristine sPEEK after 100 h along with the additive characteristics seen above in Fig. 3(FT-IR of sGNR-sGQD).

The results of the present work are compared with other similar carbon based nanostructured materials as an additive in sPEEK from important literature reports represented in Table 3 . The comparison suggests that the present nanocomposite membrane has significantly high DMFC peak power density in comparison to other carbon nanostructure based sPEEK composite electrolytes. So in conclusion, it is significant to note that the reported graphene based additive is optimized to 5 wt %, whereas in the present study, nanohybrids of optimized 1.5 wt % sGNR-sGQD is dispersed in sPEEK which is sufficient to realize higher DMFC peak power density.

The unique structural characteristics of the nanohybrid of sGNR-sGQD facilitate better dispersion in sPEEK to form nanocomposite membrane electrolytes for DMFC. The nanocomposite membrane sPEEK/sGNR-sGQD with its optimized additive content of 1.5 wt % shows improved physico-chemical properties resulting in enhanced electrochemical selectivity. Further the nanocomposite membrane shows significant enhancement in DMFC performance and better durability in comparison to pristine sPEEK and Nafion 117. These results could pave the way for the facile and efficient synthesis of sGNR-sGQD nanohybrid and further its application as polymer electrolyte membranes as an effective additive to meet the requirement of DMFC commercialization.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Mr. Avanish Shukla thank University Grant Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for the CSIR-UGC research fellowship (under UGC fellowship scheme). Dr. Santoshkumar D. Bhat thank the project under CSIR-Young Scientist Award Research Grant (DU-MLP-0090) for the funding.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.11.162.

Effect of nano-size of functionalized silica on overall performance of swelling-filling modified Nafion membrane for direct methanol fuel cell application

Innovative polymer nanocomposite electrolytes: nanoscale manipulation of ion channels by functionalized graphenes
